reason: Both answers get the core math right (LTV≈$3,200, LTV:CAC≈4:1, CAC payback≈8.3mo) and correctly flag 3% monthly churn as the weak spot investors will probe, landing on the same 'fundable but fix churn' verdict. Answer A adds a genuinely useful, decision-relevant distinction B only gestures at: logo churn vs. revenue churn, explaining that if larger accounts are the ones staying, NRR could still look strong despite high logo churn — this reframes what the founder should actually go check. A also gives more concrete next steps (get NRR ≥100%, cut logo churn to ~2%, show cohort curves) versus B's single combined suggestion. B is clean and correct but its brevity doesn't add anything A lacks, while A's logo/revenue churn distinction is new, correct, and actionable rather than padding.
